era_context: The Yuan Dynasty (Mongol rule of China) collapses as the Ming Dynasty is founded. The Hundred Years' War between England and France continues with shifting fortunes. The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia. In Mesoamerica, the Aztec Triple Alliance is expanding. This is a pivotal year for East Asian geopolitics and marks a major dynastic transition.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asian steppes (outside major trade routes)

events.title: Ming Dynasty founded; Zhu Yuanzhang proclaimed Hongwu Emperor
events.description: Zhu Yuanzhang, a former Buddhist monk and military commander, proclaims himself emperor and founds the Ming Dynasty, ending nearly a century of Mongol (Yuan) rule in China. The capital is established at Nanjing. This represents a major restoration of Han Chinese rule and marks a significant shift in Chinese governance philosophy, with the new regime emphasizing Confucian principles, centralized bureaucracy, and the restoration of Han cultural traditions. The founding of the Ming Dynasty is one of the most significant political events of the 14th century and will shape East Asian history for the next 276 years.
key_figures: Zhu Yuanzhang / Hongwu Emperor (founder of Ming Dynasty)
Toghon Temür / Emperor Shun (last Yuan emperor, fled to Mongolia)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Yuan Shih (History of the Yuan Dynasty)
Contemporary Chinese chronicles and official records

events.certainty_note: The founding of the Ming Dynasty in 1368 is one of the most thoroughly documented events in Chinese history. Multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources confirm the date and basic facts. The date corresponds to the 1st year of the Hongwu era in the Chinese calendar.

events.title: Yuan capital (Khanbaliq/Beijing) falls to Ming forces
events.description: Ming forces under Zhu Yuanzhang's command capture the Yuan capital of Khanbaliq (modern Beijing), a symbolic and strategic victory that effectively ends Yuan rule in northern China. The fall of the capital marks the culmination of years of military campaigns against Yuan forces and represents the final consolidation of Ming control over the Chinese heartland. The capture is accompanied by the flight of the Yuan emperor and court to Mongolia, where the Yuan Dynasty continues in exile for another century.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The capture of Beijing in 1368 is well-documented in multiple sources. The date is firmly established in both Chinese and Western historiography.

events.title: English forces under Edward, the Black Prince, conduct campaigns in Aquitaine
events.description: Edward, the Black Prince (eldest son of Edward III of England), leads English forces in campaigns to defend and expand English-held territories in Aquitaine during the Hundred Years' War. The campaigns involve sieges and raids against French forces and represent a continuation of English military efforts to maintain control over their continental possessions. The Black Prince's military reputation is at its height during this period, though his health is beginning to decline.

sources.name: English royal records and military dispatches

events.certainty_note: The Black Prince's military activities in 1368 are attested in contemporary chronicles, though specific campaign details may vary between sources. The Black Prince's health decline in this period is well-documented.

events.title: Urban VI becomes Pope; beginning of papal reform efforts
events.description: Bartolomeo Prignano is elected Pope and takes the name Urban VI. Urban VI is known for his reform efforts aimed at reducing corruption in the Church and reasserting papal authority. His election marks the beginning of a period of significant ecclesiastical change, though his abrasive personality and reform efforts will eventually lead to the Western Schism (1378). Urban VI's papacy represents an attempt to address growing criticism of Church corruption and clerical misconduct.
events.certainty_note: Urban VI's election in 1368 is well-documented in papal and ecclesiastical records. The date is firmly established in Church history.

events.title: Tamerlane (Timur) consolidates power in Samarkand; Central Asian dominance begins
events.description: Timur (known in the West as Tamerlane), a Turco-Mongol military leader, consolidates his control over Samarkand and the surrounding region, establishing it as his capital and power base. This marks the beginning of Timur's rise to dominance in Central Asia and sets the stage for his subsequent campaigns of conquest that will extend from the Mediterranean to India. Timur's consolidation of Central Asian power represents a significant geopolitical shift in the region.

sources.name: Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi, Zafar-nama (History of Timur)

events.certainty_note: Timur's consolidation of power in Samarkand is attested in Islamic sources, though the precise dating can vary by ±1–2 years. The Zafar-nama was compiled after Timur's death but draws on contemporary accounts.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Urban VI's election is sometimes given as 1378 rather than 1368 in older sources, but modern scholarship confirms 1368 as correct. The significance of the Black Prince's 1368 campaigns is sometimes overstated; French sources suggest English military advantage was declining by this period. Some sources question whether Timur's consolidation of Samarkand occurred in 1368 or slightly later (1369–1370), reflecting uncertainty in Central Asian chronology.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1368 is exceptionally rich for China and Europe, but sparse for other regions. Chinese sources are abundant and detailed, though they reflect the perspective of the victorious Ming Dynasty. European sources (particularly English and Italian) are well-developed. Islamic sources for Timur are available but sometimes contradictory regarding dates. African and Pacific history remain largely absent from surviving written records. The fall of the Yuan Dynasty is one of the most thoroughly documented events of the medieval period.

graph_edges.note: The founding of the Ming Dynasty directly led to the capture of the Yuan capital
Ming founding initiates the consolidation process that continues in 1369
Urban VI's reform efforts eventually lead to the Western Schism in 1378
Timur's consolidation of Samarkand enables his subsequent campaigns of conquest
